Because flusher processes are created via multiprocessing.get_context('spawn').Process, they are instances of multiprocessing.context.SpawnProcess, which on POSIX is not a subclass of multiprocessing.Process, so this isinstance check will always be false and hung processes won't be killed here.
Sleep in test_consumer.py won’t actually wait because time.sleep was monkeypatched above; consider restoring sleep or using a different sync to ensure the flusher has time to process.

true_positives.golden_comment: The queue.shutdown() method with 'immediate=False' parameter may not exist in the standard Python queue module. This could cause AttributeError at runtime. Verify the correct API or implement a custom shutdown mechanism.

The diff constructs queues via `queue.Queue()` and later calls `q.shutdown(immediate=False)` in `FixedQueuePool.shutdown()`, but stdlib `queue.Queue` has no `shutdown` method in supported versions. This will raise `AttributeError` (caught and logged), leaving worker threads potentially blocked on `get()`.
Evidence: `src/sentry/remote_subscriptions/consumers/queue_consumer.py`:
- `work_queue: queue.Queue[WorkItem[T]] = queue.Queue()`
- `q.shutdown(immediate=False)` in `FixedQueuePool.shutdown()`.

false_positives.candidate:
Uses non-existent stdlib exception `queue.ShutDown` in `OrderedQueueWorker.run()`. The diff shows `except queue.ShutDown:` in two places, but `queue` is imported from the Python standard library (`import queue`), where `ShutDown` is not available in supported versions. This will raise `AttributeError` when evaluating the except clause and breaks shutdown handling.
Evidence: `src/sentry/remote_subscriptions/consumers/queue_consumer.py`:
- `import queue`
- `except queue.ShutDown:` (twice in `OrderedQueueWorker.run`).

Shutdown can hang/leak threads: workers block on `work_queue.get()` and are only unblocked via the (invalid) `q.shutdown()`/`queue.ShutDown` mechanism. Setting `worker.shutdown = True` does not interrupt a blocking `get()`, so `worker.join(timeout=5.0)` may time out and threads keep running.

true_positives.golden_comment: The findMembers() call is now asynchronous and unhandled. The controller may not have member data immediately available, creating a race condition.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Group members page template now iterates `members` but the route sets `controller.model` to the Group and does not set a `members` property on the controller. Template: `{{#each m in members}}`; route: `controller.set("model", model); model.findMembers();`. As written, `members` will be undefined unless provided elsewhere.

true_positives.golden_comment: In the next action, capping the next offset at user_count can produce an empty page (e.g., total equal to limit results in offset == total, showing 2/2 with no members). This can cause confusing UX on the last page.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Pagination totalPages is off-by-one when user_count is an exact multiple of limit. In admin-group controller: `totalPages: function() { if (this.get("user_count") == 0) { return 0; } return Math.floor(this.get("user_count") / this.get("limit")) + 1; }` (e.g. 100/50 => 3 instead of 2). Should use ceil or equivalent.

false_positives.candidate: Admin member removal/add routes mismatch: client calls `/admin/groups/:id/members.json` but controller actions require `:group_id`. JS: `Discourse.ajax('/admin/groups/' + this.get('id') + '/members.json', ...)`; controller: `group = Group.find(params.require(:group_id).to_i)` in both `add_members` and `remove_member`. This will raise on `params.require(:group_id)`.

New admin group-member view uses an incorrect templateName that likely won’t resolve. View sets `templateName: "admin/templates/group_member"` while the template file is `app/assets/javascripts/admin/templates/group_member.hbs` (logical name would typically be `admin/group_member` or `group_member`, not including `templates`). This can prevent the item view from rendering.

Discourse.Group#findMembers now returns `undefined` when name is empty (`if (Em.isEmpty(this.get('name'))) { return ; }`), whereas it previously returned a resolved promise. Any caller that expects a promise and chains `.then(...)` can now throw at runtime.

false_negatives.golden_comment: HTTP method mismatch in .remove_member - test uses PUT but remove_member action expects DELETE

true_positives.golden_comment: BlockedEmail.should_block_email? method has side effects during a read operation - it updates statistics even when just checking if an email should be blocked. This could cause race conditions in concurrent environments and makes the method name misleading.
true_positives.matched_candidate: BlockedEmail.should_block? has a real lost-update race on `match_count`/`last_match_at` because it performs a read-modify-write in Ruby (`match_count += 1` then `save`) without atomic update/locking, so concurrent calls can undercount and overwrite timestamps. Evidence: app/models/blocked_email.rb `record.match_count += 1; record.last_match_at = Time.zone.now; record.save`.

true_positives.golden_comment: Regex pattern @(#{domains}) only matches domain suffixes, not full domains. evil.example.com would match whitelist entry example.com.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Email domain restriction regex is not anchored/boundary-checked, so it can match unintended domains/substrings (e.g., blacklist/whitelist entry `evil.com` can match `user@not-evil.com`, and `example.com` can match `example.com.evil`). Evidence: lib/validators/email_validator.rb builds `Regexp.new("@(#{domains})", true)` and tests `value =~ regexp` with no end/segment boundaries.

false_positives.candidate: Client shows the wrong error message for blocked emails: when an email is in `rejectedEmails`, the client returns a failed validation with `reason: I18n.t('user.email.invalid')` instead of using the new server-side blocked message (`user.email.blocked`). Evidence: create_account_controller.js rejectedEmails branch uses `I18n.t('user.email.invalid')`, while server adds `user.email.blocked` in EmailValidator and locale.

Email domain restriction regex does not properly escape regex metacharacters in the SiteSetting value (only `.` is escaped), so settings containing characters like `|`, `(`, `)`, `*`, `+`, etc. will change regex meaning and cause incorrect allow/deny behavior. Evidence: lib/validators/email_validator.rb: `domains = setting.gsub('.', '\\.')` then `Regexp.new("@(#{domains})", true)`.
Missing trailing newline at end of lib/validators/email_validator.rb. Evidence: diff explicitly shows `\ No newline at end of file`.

true_positives.golden_comment: -ms-align-items never existed in any version of IE/Edge; the correct legacy property is -ms-flex-align.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Invalid vendor-prefixed property in `align-items` mixin: `-ms-align-items` is not a real CSS property. The diff adds `@mixin align-items($alignment) { ... -ms-flex-align: $alignment; -ms-align-items: $alignment; ... }`, where `-ms-align-items` will be ignored (indicating a broken/incorrect cross-browser rule).

false_positives.candidate: Legacy flexbox `order` polyfill is incorrect: `-webkit-box-ordinal-group` / `-moz-box-ordinal-group` are 1-based, but the mixin passes `$val` directly. In `@mixin order($val) { -webkit-box-ordinal-group: $val; -moz-box-ordinal-group: $val; ... }` and usage `.panel { @include order(3); }`, older `-webkit-box`/`-moz-box` implementations will interpret this as ordinal-group 3 (equivalent to modern `order: 2`), causing inconsistent ordering across browsers.

Invalid legacy value for old WebKit box model: `@include align-items(baseline)` (used in `badges.css.scss` for `&.bullet`) expands to `-webkit-box-align: baseline;`, but `-webkit-box-align` (old `-webkit-box` flexbox) does not support `baseline`, so baseline alignment will not work in those legacy implementations.

false_negatives.golden_comment: Mixing float: left with flexbox causes layout issues. Further this PR removes the float-based right alignment for .d-header .panel, which may cause the login panel in the non-Ember/noscript header (where .panel is nested inside .row and not a flex item) to stack under the title instead of remaining right-aligned.

false_positives.candidate: Serializer can raise URI::InvalidURIError because only the first URI() call is rescued. In `website_name`, `website_host = URI(website.to_s).host rescue nil` is protected, but later branches call `URI(website.to_s).path` without rescue/guard. If `website` is an invalid URI string, this will raise and break serialization (500). Evidence: `website_host = URI(website.to_s).host rescue nil` followed by `website_host + URI(website.to_s).path` in multiple branches.

Potential NoMethodError if `Discourse.current_hostname` is nil/blank. `website_name` unconditionally calls `discourse_host.split('.')` and `discourse_host.ends_with?`, which will crash when `Discourse.current_hostname` returns nil. Evidence: `discourse_host = Discourse.current_hostname` then `discourse_host.split('.')` and `discourse_host.ends_with?` with no nil guard.

false_negatives.golden_comment: The include_website_name method is missing the required ? suffix. Rails serializers expect include_ methods to end with ? for conditional attribute inclusion, a convention followed by other methods in this serializer. Without it, the website_name attribute may not be conditionally included as intended. Additionally, the '.' << website_host string concatenation should be replaced with '.' + website_host or '.#{website_host}' to avoid mutating string literals, which can lead to issues.

true_positives.golden_comment: postMessage targetOrigin should be the origin (scheme+host+port), not the full referrer URL; using the full URL will cause the message to be dropped and prevent resizing.
true_positives.matched_candidate: embed layout uses an invalid postMessage targetOrigin: it passes `request.referer` (a full URL including path/query) as the targetOrigin argument to parent.postMessage, but postMessage requires an origin (scheme + host + optional port) or '*'. This will cause resize messaging to fail. Evidence: app/views/layouts/embed.html.erb: `parent.postMessage(..., '<%= request.referer %>');`

false_positives.candidate: Cook method default is set to raw_html due to enum/migration mismatch: Post.cook_methods is Enum.new(:regular, :raw_html) (so :regular=>0, :raw_html=>1), but migration adds posts.cook_method with default: 1, null: false. This makes all new posts default to :raw_html, causing Post#cook to return raw (skip cooking pipeline) for normal posts. Evidence: db/migrate/20131219203905_add_cook_method_to_posts.rb (default: 1) + app/models/post.rb (Enum.new(:regular, :raw_html) and `return raw if cook_method == Post.cook_methods[:raw_html]`).

EmbedController spec is inconsistent with controller behavior: controller enqueues Jobs.enqueue(:retrieve_topic, ...) when no TopicEmbed exists, but spec expects TopicRetriever.new(...).retrieve to be called. This will fail. Evidence: app/controllers/embed_controller.rb enqueues job; spec/controllers/embed_controller_spec.rb expects TopicRetriever to be instantiated and `retrieve` called.

PollFeed can crash when RSS items have no content: it calls `i.content.scrub` without guarding for nil, so nil content raises NoMethodError. Evidence: app/jobs/scheduled/poll_feed.rb: `content = CGI.unescapeHTML(i.content.scrub)`.

false_negatives.golden_comment: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
The current origin validation using indexOf is insufficient and can be bypassed. An attacker could use a malicious domain like evil-discourseUrl.com to pass this check.

The code sets X-Frame-Options: ALLOWALL which completely disables clickjacking protection. The referer validation can be bypassed (referer headers are easily spoofed), and the fallback to empty string for nil referer masks validation failures.

The TopicEmbed.import method is susceptible to a NoMethodError if the contents parameter is nil when attempting to append a string, and an XSS vulnerability due to unescaped url interpolation in the generated HTML.
The ERB block closes with end if, which is invalid Ruby/ERB and will raise at render; it should just be end to close the if block.

true_positives.golden_comment: The downsize method is defined twice. The second definition, which expects a single dimensions string parameter, overrides the first, which expected separate max_width and max_height parameters. This makes the original method unreachable and breaks existing code that calls it with separate width and height arguments.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Breaking API change in `OptimizedImage`: `downsize` is defined twice with different signatures, and the later definition overwrites the earlier one. In `app/models/optimized_image.rb` there is still a `def self.downsize(from, to, max_width, max_height, opts={})` (now calling optimize with `"#{max_width}x#{max_height}"`), followed by a new `def self.downsize(from, to, dimensions, opts={})`. The second definition replaces the first at runtime, so any existing callers using the 4-arg width/height API will raise `ArgumentError`.

true_positives.golden_comment: Hardcoding maxSizeKB = 10 * 1024 ignores Discourse.SiteSettings['max_' + type + '_size_kb'], so the client-side limit can diverge from server-side and per-type settings (also applies to the 413 handler below).
true_positives.matched_candidate: Client-side upload size validation is now hard-coded to 10MB, ignoring per-type SiteSettings. In `app/assets/javascripts/discourse/lib/utilities.js` the code changed from `var maxSizeKB = Discourse.SiteSettings['max_' + type + '_size_kb'];` to `var maxSizeKB = 10 * 1024; // 10MB`, so the client will enforce/show a limit that may not match configured `max_*_size_kb` values.

false_positives.candidate: The 413 (entity too large) handler now hard-codes the max size to 10MB, ignoring `Discourse.SiteSettings.max_image_size_kb`. In `utilities.js` it changed from `var maxSizeKB = Discourse.SiteSettings.max_image_size_kb;` to `var maxSizeKB = 10 * 1024; // 10 MB`, which can display an incorrect limit in the error message when the site setting differs.

API URL download limit is hard-coded to 10MB instead of respecting `SiteSetting.max_image_size_kb`. In `app/controllers/uploads_controller.rb` it changed from `FileHelper.download(url, SiteSetting.max_image_size_kb.kilobytes, ...)` to `FileHelper.download(url, 10.megabytes, ...)`, so admin configuration no longer controls this path.

UploadsController calls `OptimizedImage.downsize` with an invalid 3rd argument: it passes a quality string (`"80%"`) where the updated `OptimizedImage.downsize(from, to, dimensions, opts={})` expects an ImageMagick geometry/dimensions string (e.g. `"800x600"`). Evidence: `OptimizedImage.downsize(tempfile.path, tempfile.path, "80%", allow_animation: ...)` in `uploads_controller.rb`, and `def self.downsize(from, to, dimensions, opts={})` passing `dimensions` through to `*_instructions` in `optimized_image.rb`. This will generate invalid convert instructions and prevent downsizing from working.

false_negatives.golden_comment: Passing 80% as the dimensions can fail for animated GIFs when allow_animated_thumbnails is true, since the animated path uses gifsicle --resize-fit which expects WxH geometry, not a percentage; downsizing would then silently fail.

false_positives.candidate: Email template renders an invalid CSS class name: `app/views/email/notification.html.erb` uses `<h4 class='.previous-discussion'>...`, so the class attribute literally contains a leading dot and selectors expecting `previous-discussion` will not match.

Email unsubscribe translation now requires `%{unsubscribe_url}`, but MessageBuilder will raise if `add_unsubscribe_link: true` is used without providing `unsubscribe_url`: `config/locales/server.en.yml` adds `(%{unsubscribe_url})` and `lib/email/message_builder.rb` calls `I18n.t('unsubscribe_link', template_args)` with no default for `unsubscribe_url`.

false_negatives.golden_comment: logic: Potential nil pointer exception - if no TopicUser record exists, tu will be nil and calling methods on it will crash
Typo in property name: 'stopNotificiationsText' should be 'stopNotificationsText' (missing 'n' in 'Notifications')

true_positives.golden_comment: The updateManyByCredentialId call uses an empty data object, which prevents Prisma's @updatedAt decorator from updating the updatedAt timestamp. This results in inaccurate cache status tracking, as the timestamp isn't updated when the cache is refreshed. To fix this, explicitly set the updatedAt field.
true_positives.matched_candidate: GoogleCalendarService calls `SelectedCalendarRepository.updateManyByCredentialId(this.credential.id, {})` with an empty update `data` object. Prisma `updateMany` requires at least one field to update; passing `{}` is invalid and will throw at runtime, and it also does not accomplish the stated goal of updating `updatedAt`. Evidence: packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts and `updateManyByCredentialId(..., data)` implementation in packages/lib/server/repository/selectedCalendar.ts.

true_positives.golden_comment: logic: macOS-specific sed syntax with empty string after -i flag will fail on Linux systems
true_positives.matched_candidate: scripts/test-gcal-webhooks.sh uses macOS/BSD-specific `sed -i ''` syntax, which fails on GNU sed (common on Linux), making the script non-portable and likely to error in many environments. Evidence: `sed -i '' -E ...` in scripts/test-gcal-webhooks.sh.

false_positives.candidate: CredentialActionsDropdown hard-codes date formatting locale to "en-US", ignoring the active UI locale from useLocale(), so non-en-US users will see incorrectly localized timestamps. Evidence: `new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-US", { dateStyle: "short", timeStyle: "short" }).format(...)` in packages/features/apps/components/CredentialActionsDropdown.tsx.

CredentialActionsDropdown assumes `cacheUpdatedAt` is a Date at runtime, but it comes from tRPC/JSON and will typically be serialized (e.g., string). `new Date(cacheUpdatedAt)` can produce an invalid date and `Intl.DateTimeFormat().format(...)` will throw `RangeError: Invalid time value`. Evidence: prop type `cacheUpdatedAt?: Date | null` and usage `.format(new Date(cacheUpdatedAt))` in packages/features/apps/components/CredentialActionsDropdown.tsx; server sets `cacheUpdatedAt` from Prisma Date in connectedCalendars.handler.ts.

deleteCacheHandler throws a plain `Error` instead of a `TRPCError`, which in tRPC typically surfaces as `INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR` rather than a proper NOT_FOUND/FORBIDDEN, breaking expected API error semantics. Evidence: `throw new Error("Credential not found or access denied")` in packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/calendars/deleteCache.handler.ts.

true_positives.golden_comment: Incorrect end time calculation using slotStartTime instead of slotEndTime
true_positives.matched_candidate: Copy/paste bug in working-hours bounds check: `end` is computed from `slotStartTime` instead of the slot end time, making the end-of-slot validation ineffective. Evidence in `checkIfIsAvailable`: `const start = slotStartTime.hour() * 60 + slotStartTime.minute(); const end = slotStartTime.hour() * 60 + slotStartTime.minute();` (packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/slots.ts).

true_positives.golden_comment: Using === for dayjs object comparison will always return false as it compares object references, not values. Use .isSame() method instead: dayjs(date.start).add(utcOffset, 'minutes').isSame(dayjs(date.end).add(utcOffset, minutes))
true_positives.matched_candidate: Incorrect Dayjs equality check in date override logic: `if (dayjs(date.start).add(utcOffset, "minutes") === dayjs(date.end).add(utcOffset, "minutes"))` compares two different Dayjs object instances by reference, so it will never be true. This breaks the intended `start == end` override branch in `checkIfIsAvailable` (packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/slots.ts).

false_positives.candidate: Type contract mismatch: `checkIfIsAvailable` declares `dateOverrides?: { start: Date; end: Date; }[]`, but `getSchedule` now builds `dateOverrides` entries that include extra fields (`userId`, `timeZone`). Evidence: `availability.dateOverrides.map((override) => ({ userId: ..., timeZone: ..., ...override }))` (packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/slots.ts).

Invalid type import/export: `slots.ts` imports `WorkingHours` from `@calcom/types/schedule`, but the shown `packages/types/schedule.d.ts` does not export `WorkingHours` (it only exports `TimeRange` and `Schedule`). This will fail type-check/compilation.

true_positives.golden_comment: Using retryCount: reminder.retryCount + 1 reads a possibly stale value and can lose increments under concurrency; consider an atomic increment via Prisma (increment: 1) to avoid race conditions (also applies to the similar update in the catch block).
true_positives.matched_candidate: retryCount increment uses a stale in-memory value (reminder.retryCount + 1) instead of an atomic increment, which can lose updates under concurrent runs. Evidence: prisma.workflowReminder.update({ where: { id: reminder.id }, data: { retryCount: reminder.retryCount + 1 } }) in both the non-success branch and catch; reminder.retryCount comes from an earlier findMany result.

true_positives.golden_comment: The deletion logic in scheduleSMSReminders.ts incorrectly deletes non-SMS workflow reminders (e.g., Email, WhatsApp) that have retryCount > 1. This occurs because the retryCount condition within the OR clause for deletion lacks a method: WorkflowMethods.SMS filter, causing it to apply to all reminder types instead of only SMS reminders, which is the intended scope of this function.
true_positives.matched_candidate: DeleteMany cleanup now deletes reminders of any method when retryCount > 1 because the new OR branch is not scoped to method=SMS. Evidence: prisma.workflowReminder.deleteMany({ where: { OR: [ { method: WorkflowMethods.SMS, scheduledDate: { lte: dayjs().toISOString() } }, { retryCount: { gt: 1 } } ] } }); This can unintentionally delete non-SMS reminders (e.g., email) once their retryCount exceeds 1.

false_positives.candidate: Type contract/compilation bug: constructor now accepts `InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions` but `this.options` is typed as `InsightsBookingServiceOptions | null` and is assigned directly (`this.options = options;`). This is not assignable because the zod-derived discriminated union requires `teamId` when `scope: "team"`, while `InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions` makes `teamId` optional. Evidence: `private options: InsightsBookingServiceOptions | null;` + constructor param `options: InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions;` + assignment `this.options = options;`.

Type mismatch in internal method calls: `buildOrgAuthorizationCondition`/`buildTeamAuthorizationCondition` parameters are typed as `Extract<InsightsBookingServiceOptions, { scope: "org"|"team" }>` but are called with `this.options` which (per constructor change) is sourced from `InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions`. This is an incompatible type and will fail TypeScript (or require unsafe casts). Evidence: `return await this.buildOrgAuthorizationCondition(this.options);` / `return await this.buildTeamAuthorizationCondition(this.options);` with method signatures still using `Extract<InsightsBookingServiceOptions, ...>`.

Runtime correctness bug: `teamId` is optional in `InsightsBookingServicePublicOptions`, but `buildTeamAuthorizationCondition` uses it as required in SQL and repository calls. If a caller constructs the service with `{ scope: "team", ... }` but omits `teamId`, the code will generate SQL like `("teamId" = ${options.teamId})` and call `findByIdAndParentId({ id: options.teamId, ... })` with `undefined`, leading to incorrect filtering or runtime errors. Evidence: `teamId?: number;` and `Prisma.sql\`("teamId" = ${options.teamId}) AND ("isTeamBooking" = true)\`` plus `findByIdAndParentId({ id: options.teamId, ... })`.

false_negatives.golden_comment: In getBaseConditions(), the else if (filterConditions) and final else branches are unreachable. This is because getAuthorizationConditions() always returns a non-null Prisma.Sql object, making authConditions always truthy, which means only the first two if/else if conditions are ever evaluated.

Fetching userIdsFromOrg only when teamsFromOrg.length > 0 can exclude org-level members for orgs without child teams; consider deriving from teamIds (which includes orgId) or removing the guard so org-only orgs still include member user bookings.

true_positives.golden_comment: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
true_positives.matched_candidate: parseRefreshTokenResponse defines an invalid Zod schema using computed keys; `[z.string().toString()]` becomes a literal key (e.g. "[object Object]") and does not implement the intended catch-all validation. Evidence: `const minimumTokenResponseSchema = z.object({ access_token: z.string(), [z.string().toString()]: z.number(), [z.string().optional().toString()]: z.unknown().optional(), });` (packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts).

true_positives.golden_comment: parseRefreshTokenResponse returns a Zod safeParse result ({ success, data, error }), not the credential key object. Persisting that as key stores the wrapper instead of the token payload; we should store the parsed data or use schema parse.
true_positives.matched_candidate: parseRefreshTokenResponse returns a Zod safeParse result object, but at least one caller stores it directly as the credential `key`, persisting `{success, data}` instead of the credential payload. Evidence: `const key = parseRefreshTokenResponse(googleCredentials, googleCredentialSchema); await prisma.credential.update({ ..., data: { key }, });` (packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts) while parseRefreshTokenResponse ends with `return refreshTokenResponse;` (packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts).

true_positives.golden_comment: When APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED and CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT are set, the refreshFunction helper returns the fetch Response, but several callers (for example GoogleCalendarService.refreshAccessToken expecting res.data, and HubspotCalendarService.refreshAccessToken expecting a HubspotToken) assume it returns the integration-specific token object. That mismatch will cause runtime errors in the sync-enabled path unless the return type or those call sites are adjusted.

Evidence: refreshOAuthTokens sync branch `return response;` where `response` is from `fetch(...)` (packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts); Hubspot assigns it to `HubspotToken`: `const hubspotRefreshToken: HubspotToken = await refreshOAuthTokens(...);` (packages/app-store/hubspot/lib/CalendarService.ts); Zoho Bigin later uses `.data`: `if (!tokenInfo.data.error) { ... }` after `const tokenInfo = await refreshOAuthTokens(...)` (packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts).

true_positives.golden_comment: When the sync endpoint path is used, res is a fetch Response and has no .data; res?.data will be undefined and token.access_token will throw at runtime. This relies on a consistent return shape from refreshOAuthTokens, which isn’t guaranteed currently.
true_positives.matched_candidate: refreshOAuthTokens returns a raw Fetch `Response` when sync is enabled, but some call sites expect provider-specific objects (not a Response), causing runtime shape mismatches. Evidence: refreshOAuthTokens sync branch `return response;` where `response` is from `fetch(...)` (packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts); Hubspot assigns it to `HubspotToken`: `const hubspotRefreshToken: HubspotToken = await refreshOAuthTokens(...);` (packages/app-store/hubspot/lib/CalendarService.ts); Zoho Bigin later uses `.data`: `if (!tokenInfo.data.error) { ... }` after `const tokenInfo = await refreshOAuthTokens(...)` (packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts).

false_positives.candidate: APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED is not a boolean; it evaluates to a string (the encryption key) when both env vars are set. Evidence: `export const APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED = process.env.CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET && process.env.CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY;` (packages/lib/constants.ts).

GoogleCalendarService passes the wrong object into parseRefreshTokenResponse: it validates `googleCredentials` (mutated local credential object) instead of the refreshed token response (`res.data`). Evidence: `const token = res?.data; ... googleCredentials.access_token = token.access_token; ... const key = parseRefreshTokenResponse(googleCredentials, googleCredentialSchema);` (packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts).

Zoho Bigin CalendarService passes `credentialId` as the `userId` argument to refreshOAuthTokens, but refreshOAuthTokens uses that value as `calcomUserId` in the sync payload. This sends the wrong identifier when sync is enabled. Evidence: `refreshOAuthTokens(..., "zoho-bigin", credentialId)` (packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts) and `body: new URLSearchParams({ calcomUserId: userId.toString(), appSlug })` (packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts).

SalesforceCalendarService misuses parseRefreshTokenResponse return type: it treats the return as a safeParse result with `.success`/`.data`, but parseRefreshTokenResponse throws on failure and returns the safeParse object on success, making the `.success` check inconsistent/dead and indicating a contract mismatch. Evidence: `const accessTokenParsed = parseRefreshTokenResponse(...); if (!accessTokenParsed.success) { ... } ... { ...accessTokenParsed.data, ... }` (packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts) vs parseRefreshTokenResponse: `if (!refreshTokenResponse.success) { throw new Error(...); } return refreshTokenResponse;` (packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts).

false_negatives.golden_comment: The parseRefreshTokenResponse function incorrectly sets refresh_token to the hardcoded string 'refresh_token' when it's missing from the OAuth refresh token response. This invalidates the token, breaking subsequent token refreshes and causing authentication failures.

true_positives.golden_comment: Asynchronous functions deleteScheduledEmailReminder and deleteScheduledSMSReminder are called without await inside forEach loops. This occurs during booking rescheduling/cancellation, and workflow/workflow step deletion/updates. Consequently, scheduled workflow reminders may not be reliably cancelled, potentially leaving them active.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Multiple async reminder cancellations are fired without awaiting, so failures can be dropped and the request can complete before cancellations/DB updates finish. Evidence: `handleCancelBooking.ts` uses `forEach` and calls `deleteScheduledEmailReminder(...)` / `deleteScheduledSMSReminder(...)` without `await`; `viewer/bookings.tsx` similarly; `viewer/workflows.tsx` has `scheduledReminders.forEach(...)` without awaiting.

true_positives.golden_comment: When immediateDelete is true, the deleteScheduledEmailReminder function cancels the SendGrid email but fails to delete the corresponding WorkflowReminder record from the database. This creates orphaned database entries and is inconsistent with the immediateDelete: false path, which marks the record as cancelled. The SendGrid DELETE API call is also omitted in this path.
true_positives.matched_candidate: `deleteScheduledEmailReminder(..., immediateDelete=true)` cancels the external scheduled send and then returns without deleting or marking the `WorkflowReminder` row, leaving orphaned DB records. Evidence: `emailReminderManager.ts` -> `if (immediateDelete) { await client.request(...cancel...); return; }` (no DB update/delete in that branch).

false_positives.candidate: Email reminder cancellation no longer cancels the provider send in the common path: call sites now invoke `deleteScheduledEmailReminder(reminder.id, reminder.referenceId)` without `immediateDelete`, and the implementation only sets `cancelled: true` in DB (does not call the provider cancel API) when `referenceId` exists and `immediateDelete` is falsy. This can result in emails still being sent even though the reminder is marked cancelled. Evidence: `emailReminderManager.ts` -> `if (immediateDelete) { ... cancel ...; return; } await prisma.workflowReminder.update({ data: { cancelled: true } })`; call sites in `handleCancelBooking.ts`, `viewer/bookings.tsx`, `viewer/workflows.tsx` use the 2-arg form.

`scheduleEmailReminders.ts` cancellation query/loop does not restrict to email reminders and does not guard `referenceId` being null, yet it always calls the email provider cancel endpoint with `batch_id: reminder.referenceId`. This can attempt to cancel non-email reminders and/or send `batch_id: null` to the provider API. Evidence: `findMany({ where: { cancelled: true, scheduledDate: { lte: ... }}})` (no `method` filter) and `body: { batch_id: reminder.referenceId, status: "cancel" }` (no null check).

`viewer/workflows.tsx` uses `remindersToUpdate.forEach(async (reminder) => { ... })` but does not await the resulting promises, guaranteeing the mutation does not wait for the async work and risking unhandled rejections. Evidence: the `forEach(async ...)` block around line ~586 in the diff.

true_positives.matched_candidate: Case-sensitivity bug in blacklist and existing-attendee comparisons: blacklist entries are lowercased but incoming `guest` is not, and attendee email comparisons are case-sensitive, allowing blacklist bypass and duplicate attendees via casing differences. Evidence: packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/bookings/addGuests.handler.ts blacklist uses `.map((email) => email.toLowerCase())` but filter uses `!blacklistedGuestEmails.includes(guest)` and `guest === attendee.email` without normalizing.

true_positives.golden_comment: The logic for checking team admin/owner permissions is incorrect. This condition uses AND (&&) which requires both isTeamAdmin AND isTeamOwner to be true, but it should use OR (||) since a user needs to be either an admin OR an owner to have permission.
true_positives.matched_candidate: Team admin/owner permission check incorrectly uses AND instead of OR, requiring a user to be both team admin and team owner to pass the team-role authorization path. Evidence: packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/bookings/addGuests.handler.ts `const isTeamAdminOrOwner = (await isTeamAdmin(...)) && (await isTeamOwner(...));`.

This is backwards for a 'guests added' notification. Evidence: packages/emails/email-manager.ts `if (newGuests.includes(attendee.email)) { return sendEmail(() => new AttendeeScheduledEmail(calendarEvent, attendee)); } else { return sendEmail(() => new AttendeeAddGuestsEmail(calendarEvent, attendee)); }`.

false_negatives.golden_comment: This calls the email sender with the original guests, so existing attendees included in the input will be treated as new when sending notifications, leading to incorrect emails.

uniqueGuests filters out existing attendees and blacklisted emails but does not deduplicate duplicates within the input; createMany can insert duplicate attendee rows if the client submits repeated emails.
Starting with an array containing an empty string may cause validation issues. Consider starting with an empty array [] and handling the empty state in the MultiEmail component instead.

false_positives.candidate: EnableTwoFactorModal uses `formatBackupCode` before it is defined, which will throw at runtime when `/api/auth/two-factor/totp/setup` returns 200. Evidence: in `apps/web/components/settings/EnableTwoFactorModal.tsx`, inside `handleSetup` success path it does `body.backupCodes.map(formatBackupCode)` to build the Blob, but `const formatBackupCode = (code: string) => ...` is declared later in the component (TDZ for `const`).

false_negatives.golden_comment: The exported function TwoFactor handles backup codes and is in BackupCode.tsx. Inconsistent naming.
Error message mentions 'backup code login' but this is a disable endpoint, not login

Backup code validation is case-sensitive due to the use of indexOf(). This causes validation to fail if a user enters uppercase hex characters, as backup codes should be case-insensitive for a better user experience.

Because backupCodes are decrypted and mutated in memory before being written back, two concurrent login requests using the same backupCode could both pass this check and update, so a single backup code may effectively be accepted more than once if used concurrently, weakening the intended one-time-use semantics.

true_positives.golden_comment: The code uses forEach with async callbacks, which causes asynchronous operations (e.g., calendar/video event deletions, payment refunds) to run concurrently without being awaited. This 'fire-and-forget' behavior leads to unhandled promise rejections, race conditions, and incomplete cleanup, as surrounding try-catch blocks cannot properly handle errors from these unawaited promises. Replace forEach with for...of loops or Promise.all() with map() to ensure proper sequential execution and error handling.
